dependency_choices.reason: Native Go Starlark runtime with parser/compiler/evaluator support, thread cancellation, and execution step limits.

request.name: optional stable policy/rule id
request.input: Policy ABI object with request, history, and structured_output

response.trace: single stable trace row with rule, result, reason, and coarse span

implemented_scenarios: current-session history regex count block through regex_count_session(ctx, pattern)
fail closed on runaway loop through execution step budget and timeout cancellation
fail closed on filesystem and network attempts because no open/socket/network APIs are predeclared
safety_observations: The evaluator provides no module loader beyond a loader that always returns an error, so load/import attempts fail closed.
The Starlark thread has a max execution step budget and a wall-clock cancellation timer. The runaway-loop test proves an enabled while loop is interrupted and normalized to block.
Host filesystem and network APIs are not exposed. Attempts to call open or socket fail as undefined names and are normalized to block.
Errors are collapsed into one-line reasons with policy filename and Starlark error text; no host path, provider credential, prompt, or raw user-content logging is added.
latency_observations: No benchmark was added in this timebox.
The native test suite including Starlark scenarios completed in under one second on the local mise-managed Go toolchain.
Each evaluation currently parses and compiles policy source per call; production use would need compilation/cache strategy and benchmarked budgets.
limitations: Trace spans are coarse stable placeholders such as policy.star:evaluate, not AST source ranges.
The prototype executes source per request and does not implement policy signing, persistence, cache invalidation, or multi-tenant isolation boundaries beyond the Starlark sandbox.
The regex helper is intentionally narrow and only counts current-session history text against ctx.request.metadata.session.
The endpoint is under /__test and is not wired into chat routing or receipt production flow.

architecture: Acceptable for spike: the evaluator is isolated from existing governance policy code and exposed only through a deterministic test endpoint.

Production blocker: parsing source on every call and accepting arbitrary source directly over HTTP is not a deployable architecture without authenticated policy management, compiled policy caching, versioning, and route integration.
code_quality: The conversion layer is deliberately small and explicit for JSON-compatible ABI types.
The only custom predeclared helper is regex_count_session; it should not grow into an unstructured standard library without a reviewed ABI.
security: No imports, filesystem, network, subprocess, environment, or provider access are exposed to policies.
Fail-closed normalization covers runtime errors, syntax/load errors, invalid action results, missing switch_model model, and runaway execution.
test_quality: Tests assert externally visible policy decisions and reasons, not internal Starlark implementation details.
Negative tests are capable of failing: they require block actions and specific fail-closed evidence for errors, sandbox attempts, invalid results, and runaway loops.

viability_verdict.summary: Native Go Starlark is viable for deterministic policy execution and fail-closed sandbox behavior in the Go prototype. The main remaining work is production policy lifecycle, compiled-policy caching, trace/source-span enrichment, and integration into the real routing/receipt path.
